steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content:
The sentence reads: "a team of researchers ______ to find connections between the physical location in which a social media post was created and the content of that post analyzed geotagged social media posts."

The word analyzed is already the main verb. The blank must be filled by a non-finite form that modifies the subject without competing with analyzed as a second main verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'were hoping': a finite past-progressive verb. This creates two competing main verbs ("were hoping" and "analyzed") with no conjunction connecting them — a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — 'was hoping': a finite past-progressive verb (singular). Like A, this creates two competing main verbs ("was hoping" and "analyzed") with no conjunction connecting them — a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — 'hoped': a finite simple-past verb, producing the same run-on: "a team hoped ... analyzed ..." with no connector. ✗
  • Choice D — 'hoping': a present participle. It creates a participial phrase "hoping to find connections ..." that modifies the subject while leaving analyzed as the sole main verb. Grammatically correct. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
